o: On-demand delivery of IT resources over the internet with pay-as-you-go pricing
Using a personal computer for compute
Storing files on a USB drive
e: Cloud computing = on-demand IT resources via internet with pay-as-you-go.

q: Which is a benefit of cloud computing over on-premises?
o: Trade capital expense for variable expense
e: Cloud eliminates large upfront capital expenses, replacing with variable operating costs.

o: Ability to automatically scale resources up or down based on demand
e: Elasticity = automatic scaling to match demand. Scale out when busy, scale in when quiet.

q: Which is a design principle of the AWS Well-Architected Framework?
o: Stop guessing capacity and use auto-scaling
e: Well-Architected: stop guessing capacity, test systems at production scale, automate.

q: What are the six pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework?
o: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability
Compute, Storage, Network, Database, Security, Identity
EC2, S3, RDS, Lambda, VPC, IAM
Cost, Speed, Size, Color, Shape, Weight
e: Six pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability.

q: What is the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF)?
o: Guidance to help organizations plan cloud migration across six perspectives
e: CAF: Business, People, Governance, Platform, Security, Operations perspectives.

q: Which cloud deployment model runs entirely on AWS?
e: Cloud-native = fully on AWS. Hybrid = mix of on-prem and cloud.

q: What is a hybrid cloud deployment?
o: Infrastructure that connects on-premises resources with cloud resources
e: Hybrid connects existing on-prem infrastructure with AWS cloud resources.

q: Which benefit means you can deploy globally in minutes?
e: AWS has Regions worldwide. Deploy in multiple geographies in minutes.

q: 'Benefit from massive economies of scale' means:
o: AWS aggregates usage from many customers resulting in lower prices
e: AWS aggregates hundreds of thousands of customers = lower pay-as-you-go prices.

o: Speed of provisioning resources and iterating, reducing time from idea to implementation
e: Agility: new resources clicks away, experiment quickly, fail fast, iterate.

q: Which migration strategy involves moving applications as-is to the cloud?
e: Rehost = lift and shift. Move without changes. Fastest migration path.

q: Which migration strategy means rewriting the application for cloud-native?
e: Refactor = re-architect for cloud-native features like serverless, microservices.

o: System remains operational and accessible with minimal downtime
e: HA = system stays running despite failures. Multiple AZs, redundancy, failover.

o: System continues operating correctly even when components fail
e: Fault tolerant: component fails but system keeps working. Built-in redundancy.

q: What is disaster recovery in AWS?
o: Strategies and services to recover IT infrastructure after a disruption
e: DR: backup, multi-region, pilot light, warm standby, multi-site strategies.

o: Pay only for resources you actually consume with no upfront commitment
e: Pay only for what you use. No long-term contracts required. Stop anytime.

q: Which describes Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)?
o: You manage OS, apps, data; AWS manages hardware, network, virtualization
e: IaaS (EC2): you control OS and above. AWS handles physical infrastructure.

q: Which describes Platform as a Service (PaaS)?
o: AWS manages infrastructure and runtime; you manage code and data
e: PaaS (Elastic Beanstalk): deploy code, AWS handles infrastructure.

q: Which describes Software as a Service (SaaS)?
o: Fully managed application accessed via browser; vendor manages everything
e: SaaS: complete application. No infrastructure management. Just use it.

o: A physical location with multiple isolated Availability Zones
e: Region = geographic area with 2+ AZs. Data stays in region unless you move it.

o: One or more discrete data centers with redundant power, networking, and connectivity within a Region
e: AZ = isolated data center(s) within a Region. Connected via low-latency links.

q: What are edge locations used for?
o: Caching content closer to users for lower latency (CloudFront)
e: Edge locations: CloudFront CDN, Route 53 DNS. Content closer to users.

q: Why would you choose a specific AWS Region?
o: Compliance, latency, available services, and pricing vary by region
e: Region selection: data residency laws, proximity to users, service availability, cost.

q: What is the AWS Free Tier?
o: Offers free usage of certain services for 12 months, always free, or trials
e: Free Tier: 12-month free (EC2, S3), always free (Lambda, DynamoDB), trials.

q: Which is NOT a benefit of cloud computing?
e: Shared responsibility means you still have operational duties. Cloud doesn't eliminate all responsibility.

q: What does 'stop spending money running data centers' mean?
o: Focus on business differentiation instead of managing physical infrastructure
e: Cloud eliminates undifferentiated heavy lifting of managing physical data centers.

o: Increasing the size/capacity of a single resource (bigger instance)
e: Vertical = scale up (bigger instance). Horizontal = scale out (more instances).

o: Adding more instances to distribute load
e: Horizontal = add more instances. Auto Scaling Groups enable this automatically.

q: Which AWS service helps plan migration from on-premises?
e: Migration Hub: central location to track migration progress across AWS tools.

o: Making targeted optimizations during migration without changing core architecture
e: Replatform (lift, tinker, shift): minor optimizations like moving DB to RDS.

o: Identifying and decommissioning applications no longer needed
e: Retire: turn off things you don't need. Reduces cost and attack surface.

o: Keeping certain applications on-premises for now due to complexity or compliance
e: Retain: not ready to migrate yet. Keep on-prem, revisit later.

q: What does 'operational excellence' pillar focus on?
o: Running and monitoring systems to deliver business value and improve processes
e: Operational Excellence: automate changes, respond to events, define standards.

q: What does the 'reliability' pillar focus on?
o: Ability of a system to recover from disruptions and meet demand
e: Reliability: auto-recover from failure, test recovery, scale horizontally, stop guessing capacity.

q: What does the 'sustainability' pillar focus on?
o: Minimizing environmental impact of cloud workloads
e: Sustainability: right-size resources, use efficient architectures, reduce waste.

o: Infrastructure as Code service that provisions resources using templates
e: CloudFormation: define infrastructure in JSON/YAML templates. Repeatable, version-controlled.

q: What is a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)?
o: A logically isolated virtual network you define within AWS
e: VPC: your isolated section of AWS cloud. Control IP ranges, subnets, routing, security.

q: What does 'loose coupling' mean in cloud architecture?
o: Components interact through well-defined interfaces minimizing dependencies
e: Loose coupling: reduce interdependencies. If one component fails, others continue. Use queues, APIs.

q: What is the AWS Shared Responsibility Model?
o: AWS secures infrastructure (OF the cloud); customer secures data and configuration (IN the cloud)
e: AWS: physical, network, hypervisor. Customer: data, IAM, encryption, patching, firewall rules.

o: Service to manage users, groups, roles, and permissions for AWS access
e: IAM: create users, groups, roles. Attach policies to control who can do what.

q: What is the principle of least privilege?
o: Grant only the minimum permissions needed to perform a task
e: Least privilege: start with zero permissions, add only what's needed. Reduces blast radius.

o: Multiple Factor Authentication adding an extra layer of security beyond passwords
e: MFA: something you know (password) + something you have (device/token). Strongly recommended for root.

q: What is the AWS root user?
o: The account owner with unrestricted access to all AWS services and resources
e: Root user: full access. Use MFA, don't use for daily tasks. Create IAM users instead.

o: An identity with permissions that can be assumed temporarily by users, services, or applications
e: Roles: temporary credentials. EC2 assumes role for S3 access. No hardcoded keys.

o: Service to centrally manage multiple AWS accounts with consolidated billing and policies
e: Organizations: central management, SCPs, consolidated billing, account grouping via OUs.

q: What is a Service Control Policy (SCP)?
o: Policy in AWS Organizations that sets permission guardrails for member accounts
e: SCPs: maximum permissions boundary for accounts/OUs. Even if IAM allows it, SCP can deny it.

o: Service that logs all API calls and account activity for auditing and compliance
e: CloudTrail: who did what, when, from where. Every API call logged. Essential for auditing.

o: Intelligent threat detection service that continuously monitors for malicious activity
e: GuardDuty: ML-based threat detection. Analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs.
Shield Standard: free, auto DDoS protection. Shield Advanced: paid, enhanced protection + support.

o: Web Application Firewall that filters HTTP/HTTPS traffic based on rules
e: WAF: block SQL injection, XSS, rate limiting, geo-blocking. Protects CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway.

o: Protecting stored data using encryption when not actively being used
e: At rest: S3 SSE, EBS encryption, RDS encryption. Data protected while stored.

o: Key Management Service for creating and managing encryption keys
e: KMS: create, rotate, manage encryption keys. Integrates with S3, EBS, RDS, etc.

o: Virtual firewall for EC2 instances controlling inbound and outbound traffic at instance level
e: Security Groups: stateful, allow rules only, instance-level. Default: deny all inbound, allow all outbound.
SG: stateful (return traffic auto-allowed), instance-level. NACL: stateless, subnet-level.

o: On-demand access to AWS compliance reports and security documentation
e: Artifact: download SOC, PCI, ISO reports. Self-service compliance document access.

q: Which TWO are customer responsibilities? (Select TWO)
e: Customer patches EC2 OS and configures Security Groups. AWS handles physical infra.

o: Centralized view of security alerts and compliance status across AWS accounts
e: Security Hub: aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, Config. Single pane of glass.

o: Virtual servers (instances) in the cloud with resizable compute capacity
e: EC2: launch virtual servers. Choose instance type, OS, storage. Pay per second/hour.

o: Object storage service with unlimited scalability, 99.999999999% durability
e: S3: store any amount of data. Objects in buckets. 11 nines durability.

o: Serverless compute that runs code in response to events without managing servers
e: Lambda: upload code, runs on events. Pay per request + duration. No servers to manage.

o: Managed relational database service supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB
e: RDS: managed DB. AWS handles patching, backups, scaling. Multi-AZ for HA.

o: Fully managed NoSQL key-value and document database with single-digit millisecond performance
e: DynamoDB: serverless NoSQL. Auto-scales. Single-digit ms latency at any scale.

o: Global Content Delivery Network (CDN) that caches content at edge locations for low latency
e: CloudFront: CDN. Cache at 400+ edge locations. Faster delivery, lower load on origin.

o: Automatically distributes incoming traffic across multiple targets (EC2, containers, IPs)
e: ELB: distribute traffic. ALB (HTTP), NLB (TCP), GLB (third-party appliances).

o: Scalable DNS web service for domain registration, routing, and health checking
e: Route 53: DNS. Domain registration, routing policies (latency, geolocation, failover).

o: Automatically adjusts number of EC2 instances based on demand or schedule
e: Auto Scaling: add/remove instances automatically. Maintain availability, optimize cost.

o: Block storage volumes that attach to EC2 instances (virtual hard drives)
e: EBS: persistent block storage for EC2. Snapshots for backup. SSD and HDD options.

o: S3=objects/unlimited; EBS=block/single EC2; EFS=file/shared across EC2s
e: S3: objects, unlimited. EBS: block, attached to one EC2. EFS: NFS, shared across instances.

o: PaaS that deploys and manages applications; you upload code, it handles infrastructure
e: Beanstalk: deploy web apps. Auto-handles capacity, load balancing, scaling, monitoring.

o: Fully managed message queuing service for decoupling components
e: SQS: send, store, receive messages between components. Decouples microservices.

o: Fully managed pub/sub messaging service for sending notifications
e: SNS: publish messages to subscribers (email, SMS, Lambda, SQS, HTTP).

o: Fully managed data warehouse for running analytics queries on petabytes of data
e: Redshift: data warehouse. Columnar storage. SQL analytics on structured data at scale.

o: Dedicated private network connection from your premises to AWS (not over internet)
e: Direct Connect: private dedicated fiber connection. Consistent performance, lower bandwidth cost.

o: Monitoring and observability service for metrics, logs, alarms, and dashboards
e: CloudWatch: collect metrics, set alarms, view logs, create dashboards. Monitor everything.

o: Serverless query service to analyze data in S3 using standard SQL
e: Athena: query S3 data with SQL. No infrastructure. Pay per query. Great for ad-hoc analysis.

o: Serverless compute for containers; run containers without managing servers or clusters
e: Fargate: serverless containers. No EC2 management. Works with ECS and EKS.

o: Fully managed container orchestration service to run Docker containers
e: ECS: run containers on AWS. Manages scheduling, scaling. Use with Fargate or EC2.

o: Physical devices for offline data transfer and edge computing (Snowcone, Snowball, Snowmobile)
e: Snow: physical data transfer. Snowcone (8TB), Snowball Edge (80TB), Snowmobile (100PB).

o: Fully managed service to build, train, and deploy machine learning models
e: SageMaker: complete ML platform. Build, train, deploy. Notebooks, training, endpoints.
Lambda is serverless. EC2 requires you to manage instances. EBS is block storage.

o: Allows instances in private subnets to access the internet while remaining unreachable from internet
e: NAT Gateway: private subnet instances reach internet for updates. Internet can't initiate to them.

o: Easy-to-use service for simple web applications with fixed monthly pricing
e: Lightsail: simple VPS. Fixed pricing. Good for small websites, dev environments.

o: AWS infrastructure and services delivered to your on-premises location
e: Outposts: run AWS services in your data center. Same APIs, same tools. For low-latency or data residency.

o: Service to collect, process, and analyze real-time streaming data
e: Kinesis: real-time data streaming. IoT, clickstreams, logs. Process data as it arrives.

o: Service to build conversational chatbots using voice and text
e: Lex: same tech as Alexa. Build chatbots for apps. Speech recognition + NLU.

o: Serverless ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) service for data preparation
e: Glue: serverless ETL. Discover, transform, load data for analytics.

o: Fully managed service to create, publish, and manage APIs at any scale
e: API Gateway: create REST/WebSocket APIs. Throttling, auth, caching. Integrates with Lambda.

o: Serverless orchestration service to coordinate multiple AWS services into workflows
e: Step Functions: visual workflows. Coordinate Lambda, ECS, SageMaker. Error handling built in.

o: Serverless event bus for building event-driven applications
e: EventBridge: route events between AWS services, SaaS apps, custom apps.

o: Cloud-native business intelligence (BI) service for creating visualizations and dashboards
e: QuickSight: serverless BI. Dashboards, ML insights, embedded analytics. Pay per session.

o: MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible relational database with 5x MySQL performance and high availability
e: Aurora: cloud-native relational DB. 5x MySQL, 3x PostgreSQL. Auto-scales, multi-AZ.

o: Managed in-memory caching service (Redis or Memcached) for microsecond latency
e: ElastiCache: in-memory cache. Redis/Memcached. Microsecond reads. Reduce DB load.

o: Operational hub to view and manage AWS infrastructure (patching, automation, parameter store)
e: Systems Manager: patch management, run commands, parameter store, session manager.

o: Fully managed graph database service for highly connected datasets
e: Neptune: graph DB. Social networks, recommendation engines, fraud detection.

q: What are the three AWS pricing models for compute?
o: On-Demand, Reserved Instances/Savings Plans, Spot Instances
e: On-Demand: pay per hour/second. Reserved/Savings: commit for discount. Spot: up to 90% off, interruptible.

o: Commitment to use a specific instance type for 1 or 3 years in exchange for significant discount
e: Reserved: 1 or 3 year commitment. Up to 72% discount. All Upfront, Partial, No Upfront options.

o: Spare EC2 capacity at up to 90% discount that can be interrupted with 2-minute warning
e: Spot: unused capacity. Up to 90% off. Can be reclaimed. Good for fault-tolerant workloads.

o: Flexible pricing model offering lower prices in exchange for consistent usage commitment ($/hour for 1-3 years)
e: Savings Plans: commit to $/hour usage. More flexible than Reserved. Compute or EC2 Instance plans.

q: What is the AWS Pricing Calculator?
o: Tool to estimate costs for AWS architectures before deploying
e: Pricing Calculator: model your solution, estimate monthly cost. Free to use.

o: Tool to visualize, understand, and manage AWS costs and usage over time
e: Cost Explorer: historical spending graphs, forecasts, filter by service/region/tag.

o: Service to set custom spending thresholds and receive alerts when costs exceed them
e: Budgets: set cost/usage thresholds. Get alerts via email/SNS. Can trigger actions.

o: Single bill for all accounts in AWS Organizations with potential volume discounts
e: Consolidated billing: one bill for org. Aggregate usage = volume discounts.

q: What are resource tags for billing?
o: Key-value pairs assigned to resources to organize and track costs by project, team, or environment
e: Tags: assign metadata. Filter Cost Explorer by tag. Enforce tagging for cost allocation.

q: Which AWS Support plan includes a Technical Account Manager (TAM)?
e: Enterprise Support includes TAM, Concierge, 15-min response for critical issues.

q: What are the AWS Support plans?
o: Basic (free), Developer, Business, Enterprise On-Ramp, Enterprise
e: Basic=free. Developer=$29+. Business=$100+. Enterprise On-Ramp=$5,500. Enterprise=$15,000+.

q: What is the Basic Support plan?
o: Free plan with 24/7 access to documentation, whitepapers, Trusted Advisor core checks, and Health Dashboard
e: Basic: free. Self-service. Trusted Advisor (7 core checks). Personal Health Dashboard.

o: Pay for storage (per GB), requests (per 1000), and data transfer out
e: S3 cost: storage GB + requests + data transfer out. Different storage classes = different prices.

o: Data IN is free; data OUT to internet is charged; between AZs has a small charge
e: In = free. Out to internet = charged (tiered). Between AZs = small charge. Same AZ = free.

o: Service that recommends optimal EC2 instance types based on actual usage patterns
e: Compute Optimizer: analyzes utilization. Recommends right-size instances. Reduce cost, improve perf.

o: Digital catalog of third-party software that can be purchased and deployed on AWS
e: Marketplace: buy/sell third-party software. AMIs, containers, SaaS. Consolidated billing.

o: Service providing real-time guidance on cost, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits
e: Trusted Advisor: five categories. Full access with Business/Enterprise Support.

q: What is the Concierge Support team?
o: Dedicated billing and account experts included with Enterprise Support
e: Concierge: Enterprise Support only. Help with billing, account, best practices for cost.
